To explain my position here, Python doesn't allow inequality-based comparisons between complex numbers. MATLAB allows it but disregards the imaginary part when making the comparison. There's no viable course of action where we can allow complex-valued inequality constraints and not end up confusing the heck out of some people. It's better not to support this and fail loudly than to risk hidden bugs for a tiny bit of syntactic sugar.

1. close https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4175

```shell
git checkout master

3. Open a new pull request for the `gcc-tips` documentation, from the branch `docs/gcc-alt-txt`
4. reset `master` locally and on your fork to LightGBM's `master`

```shell
# delete local master branch
git branch -D master

# add LightGBM official repo as another remo
git remote add upstream git@github.com:microsoft/LightGBM.git

# get master branch from LightGBM official repo
git fetch upstream master
git checkout master

# overwrite your fork's "master" branch with the official LightGBM one
git push origin master --force
```

5. Rebase this branch to that new `master`

```shell
git checkout akshita_fstrings
git rebase master
git push origin akshita_fstrings --force
```

> Casper, could you assess/prototype the feasibility of that approach with your VNA?

I tried and it seems to work. 

Additionally, I just found something interesting to using `format_map` rather than the normal `format` in general: apparently its a bit faster (see [this](https://stackoverflow.com/a/36780792) explanation), and you don't per se need the `PartialFormatDict`: if you format the command in your nested channel like this `"SOUR{{ch}}:POW:PORT{pt}?"` (note the double `{{` and `}}` around ch), format_map(pt=1) will return `"SOUR{ch}:POW:PORT1?"` (note the single `{` and `}` around ch). We could use this to a) keep the core simple for simple devices (because I don't see a reason not to use `format_map` rather than `format`) and b) not have to do anything special (except document this approach) for more complex, nested channels. Only thing we need then is the following `replace_placeholder` method and a `placeholder` attribute:

``` python
    def replace_placeholder(self, command):
        return command.format_map({self.placeholder: self.id})
```

According to the signature of the `ImmutableMultiDict`'s `__init__` method:

```python
class ImmutableMultiDict(typing.Mapping[_KeyType, _CovariantValueType]):
    _dict: typing.Dict[_KeyType, _CovariantValueType]

    def __init__(
        self,
        *args: typing.Union[
            "ImmutableMultiDict[_KeyType, _CovariantValueType]",
            typing.Mapping[_KeyType, _CovariantValueType],
            typing.Iterable[typing.Tuple[_KeyType, _CovariantValueType]],
        ],
        **kwargs: typing.Any,
    ) -> None:
```

We should parametrize these ones as `[str, str]`, but the following happens a few lines underneath:

```python
self._list = [(str(k), str(v)) for k, v in self._list]
self._dict = {str(k): str(v) for k, v in self._dict.items()}
```

At least for the current set of schemes, the scheme alone is enough to dispatch. Using scheme alone here would make it easier to deprecate the redundant keytype field.

Here is the quick rundown of what is happening:

Currently, if you put annotations on an object, they are stored as actual objects:

```python
def foo(bar: int, baz: float) -> str:
    pass

print(foo.__annotations__)
```

```
{'bar': <class 'int'>, 'baz': <class 'float'>, 'return': <class 'str'>}
```

This is problematic if you need to annotate with something that is not completely defined yet. A very common case (and the one we are hitting here), is a return annotation on a `@classmethod`. For example

```python
class Foo:
    def __new__(cls) -> Foo:
        pass
```

```
NameError: name 'Foo' is not defined
```

The workaround is to stringify the annotation. Since that is quite ugly, there is this line

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/fc83b280387d92fe6aea73f35c1ed4ad1bb739da/torchvision/datapoints/_bounding_box.py#L1

that enables this behavior automagically. For example

```python
from __future__ import annotations

def foo(bar: int, baz: float) -> str:
    pass

print(foo.__annotations__)

class Foo:
    def __new__(cls) -> Foo:
        pass
```

```
{'bar': 'int', 'baz': 'float', 'return': 'str'}
```

This behavior is supposed to become mandatory in a future release of Python, but so far that was [postponed multiple times](https://docs.python.org/3/library/__future__.html#id1).

---

Coming back the test: to be able to compare the annotations, we need to make sure that we either have the proper types on both objects or the corresponding strings.

For this to work in general, all `Content` types need to have `keys` defined, and they need to be consistent about whether it's a method or a property. This implementation assumes that `self._contents[i]` has a `keys` property, which won't work if `self._contents[i]` is a UnionArray because you just defined this `keys` as a method. (No parentheses.) I think RecordArray has a `keys` property, so that will require all `Content` subclasses to have a `keys` property, not method.

The output should be a list. I don't think it was a set in the past—that would cause problems with Python 2.7 and 3.5, which don't have stable set order. I also think the `keys` of a Union _ought_ to be the intersection, rather than the union (as above), since the only keys one could pass to `_getitem_field` or `_getitem_fields` are the ones that exist on all `contents` of the UnionArray. Maybe it wasn't originally designed that way (in v1), but we have more experience with UnionArrays now: they're only useful in operations that assume the least common denominator of the `contents`, which would be an intersection here. Any test instances that require the output to be the union can be changed or deleted.

Independent of the above, this is a method that can be (and therefore should be) implemented on the Form subclasses, rather than the Content subclasses. What it returns only depends on the metadata, which is in the Form. By defining it on Forms, VirtualArray will be able to use it without unnecessarily reading data. That makes this method like `minmax_depth`, `purelist_depth`, and a few others that I implemented on the Forms a week or two ago. The Content implementations just link to the Form implementations, using a Python trick. This `keys` property should eventually become like that, but if you want to just delete the test instances that need it and move on, we can do it later.

```python
import numpy
import scipy
from scipy import ndimage

from scipy import misc

numpy.set_printoptions(linewidth=120)

print("\nscipy.ndimage.zoom grid_mode=False:\n", y, sep='')

# ✔scipy.ndimage.zoom grid_mode = True - Suitable. Aligns half pixels.

# ✔Suitable.

Oh, I see now. You are simply overriding `uint8_t` with other one with preprocessor macros. I'm afraid this solution is not scalable, because if you introduce a new crypto engine implementation, you would need to modify interface to support other key attributes type.

I think the best solution to keep implementation-specific data would be to define `otCryptoKeyAttributes` as an array like:
```
typedef uint8_t otCryptoKeyAttributes[OT_PLAT_CRYPTO_KEY_ATTRIBUTES_SIZE];
```
and override the size during building process, instead of the type itself.

Anyway when thinking about it some more I am not sure Django REST Framework meant the `get_serializer` method to raise any API exception even when it is called several times (Of course it could always throw an random error which will end up as 500 but those exceptions are not handled in our handler). The reason is that the serializer defines the schema and `get_serializer` should return a valid serializer without a specific request for this to work. This is used by features like openapi etc. Sometimes even in the DRF code `get_serializer` gets called twice (see [here](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/8812394ed83d7cce0ed5b2c5fcf093269d364b9b/rest_framework/schemas/openapi.py#L232)).

So as initially [stated](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/pull/1051#discussion_r802963490) it might make sense to catch handled exceptions (APIException etc.). Other 500 errors which could be caused when `get_serializer` is called several times in my opinion need to be handled in the client code. And changing to `get_serializer_class` is not a way forward because it breaks DRF compatibility. Features like [dynamic modification of fields](https://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/serializers/#dynamically-modifying-fields) which `get_serializer` is a good spot to do would not work properly.

This is of course my opinion and I am very open to discuss this more. For this a PR is not ideal and it would best to [open a discussion](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/discussions/new) to get more people involved.

Yep, that is a cleaner API from the user perspective. I was trying to keep the extra work that the python client is doing to a minimum so that it is more of a basic client to interact with the API as it is defined, which requires working with the `execution_id`. Requiring the user to keep track of the execution id is also how the API for the Athena client works. I guess this is more of a philosophical difference as to what a client should be doing. I prefer the minimalist approach so that the language-specific clients are a simple wrapper over the actual API. That said, offering a more intuitive interface is more consistent how the rest of the python client works.

Some day, I'd like to take the AWS approach and have two ways to interact with our APIs: one low-level client interface that is this really thin wrapper around the API and then a higher-level interface for interacting with resources on the platform via language-specific objects. It would make it easier to auto-generate the lower-level APIs based on swagger or some other IDL. I know this separation exists in the python client today, we just don't give users explicit access to the lower-level api.

As far as I can tell `run_open` uses the `edit` implementation from click's `termui`.
https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/55856c70ace5caf7c1b53d253fbc7c4fa7421ac0/pipenv/vendor/click/termui.py#L481

This function has several optional arguments including the `env` argument which is where the user passes in environment variables they want to pass into the editor. By default all the arguments are set to `None` so by default the environment variables are not passed in to the `Editor` object.

I can confirm that Click does not automatically forward `os.environ`. 

You can find the code that handles the environment variables [here](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/b9a72b4b234b4a8090c7ef085375658ee12aaffe/click/_termui_impl.py#L421-L425) on lines 421-425 of `_termui_impl.py`. `os.environ` and it's contents is only passed to the editor if the `Editor`'s `env` variable is defined in which case it copies the contents of `os.environ` appends it's own `env` variables and passes that into the editor. If it's not the case it passes `None` to the editor's environment. 

This does sound like a bug on Click's side. Should we close this PR and I'll create a PR to Click fixing the bug?

> That is to say, if we threw an exception and continued, would it be possible for more user code to run?

It would be dangerous for us to do so, because 1) if a user caught the exception and handled it somehow, they would have a partially-initialized Resource object whose output properties would never be resolved. Any `apply` on them would deadlock and all future resource operations using those outputs would also deadlock waiting for resolution. In my eyes, it's much safer in this case to exit.

Here the PR tries to compact the list of parameters of the `replace` function, making it a shorter definition. Since the param list is getting long, it's understandable. However only using variadic parameters (`*args`) means that the function interface is not known to the caller anymore. So [inspect](https://docs.python.org/3/library/inspect.html) or [`replace.__code__.co_varnames`](https://python-reference.readthedocs.io/en/latest/docs/code/varnames.html#co-varnames) won't return the actual function parameters anymore, and editors won't be able to autocomplete when calling the function.

Maybe we can document the params list (with a function doc), I'm also OK with having a long function param list.
It's self documenting, and not that unusual in Python libraries. For example some famous projects keep long function parameters, like [pika](https://github.com/pika/pika/blob/160caa4e4621711290a915eec5f4a34c01ea2d1b/pika/spec.py#L696), [boto](https://github.com/boto/boto/blob/eca5f987aa59ec7490aa91466853c36b0cf40b3c/boto/pyami/installers/__init__.py#L30), [ansible](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/40e5d2c951d5bc35d98fc47f66557713789f65c1/lib/ansible/plugins/callback/__init__.py#L380), [pycparser](https://github.com/eliben/pycparser/blob/2a29d56c280e64b308cd079b507ebee3e108fddc/pycparser/c_ast.py#L383).

> Why should we allow other fields than the ones defined?

That’s not what the `*` does, it’s not the same as `*<variable name>` (e.g. `*args`). A single `*` as a parameter simply causes all parameters after it to require to be passed by name. See https://python-3-for-scientists.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python3_advanced.html#keyword-only-arguments

So, before this change, you can create an instance of `DateData` class 2 different ways:

```python
DateData(date_object, 'day', 'gl_ES', False)
DateData(date_object=date_object, period='day', locale='gl_ES', is_relative=True)
```

With my suggestion, only the latter works.

With classes and functions where positional parameter are not obvious, keyword-only arguments are a way to force calling code to remain readable.

I don’t have a strong opinion on this, though. Feel free to ignore and mark as resolved.

the reason why requirements.txt is in project root while setup.py is at the repo level is for the multi repo case (example: https://github.com/yuhan/my-dagster-project-multiple-locations), we'll have the requirements.txt at the root which installs all the dagster repos / code locations as separate python packages: 
```
.
├── README.md
├── cloud_workspace.yaml
├── my_repo_1
│   ├── my_repo_1
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── assets
│   │   │   └── __init__.py
│   │   └── repository.py
│   ...
│   ├── pyproject.toml
│   ├── setup.cfg
│   └── setup.py
├── my_repo_2
│   ├── my_repo_2
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── assets
│   │   │   └── __init__.py
│   │   └── repository.py
│   ...
│   ├── pyproject.toml
│   ├── setup.cfg
│   └── setup.py
├── requirements.txt
```

Currently, we want the requirements.txt in the git repo root because that's how the cloud cli assumes.

Well, ok. In my tests, running both --with-scheduler and rq-scheduler doesn't end up well.
By returning False, I'm basically _disabling_ the feature, since it won't be shown in the dashboard.

After Some digging I found that, for some reason, rq and rq-scheduler organize scheduling and enqueuing of scheduled jobs in parallel ways, namely incompatible.

Please correct me if I'm wrong: Basically, jobs scheduled using Scheduler.enqueue_at will never be picked up by a worker --with-scheduler (*different* redis keys), and also the opposite is true. I wouldn't understand anyone having both running (and if they do, the worker will be worker only).

Recently, @cunla's django-rq-scheduler (>2022.12.1) transitioned to --with-scheduler.

My point is: showing that the Scheduler is active (worker --with-scheduler) and then scheduling jobs using rq-scheduler enqueue_at will cause stale jobs that never run and are never seen by django-rq.

What I propose is your original idea but only working if the rq-scheduler is not to be found, otherwise, the information would be misleading, to say the least.

This now looks to me like we're getting dangerously close to re-implementing `LegacyVersion` as `_NonVersion`. It seems to me if packaging is removing support for the Legacy Version, then probably Setuptools should do so too. Here's what I'd like to happen:

- in the Packaging project (or possibly on another forum like packaging-problems or distutils-sig), discuss the deprecation and removal of LegacyVersion and what they intend to happen in cases where packages still use those versions.
- Follow the spirit of that change. For example, if they're just testing the waters to see who complains about the deprecation, we should complain and explain this use case that's broken by the change. If they have a plan to wean users off of LegacyVersions by dropping support for them, then we should honor that intent and wean our users off of them as well.

I don't want setuptools to be the dumping ground for legacy behavior. If users want that behavior but the PyPA wishes to dissuade that behavior, Setuptools would like to honor that intent.

The TTLs were actually an anti-optimization in practice (except for Twitter's hack to reduce load on find-links repos). Any positive TTL causes Pip to ignore its cache once the TTL expires whereas a 0 TTL causes it to use the cache with a conditional GET as long as the resource stays the same. So this appears to be a strict win except for the Twitter case. I've updated #1084 with some comments about this upgrade. Don't use friend functions for this.  The utility of having these things as global functions is that they don't complicate the interface of `fft_size`.  Classes should be minimal and complete. Then higher level functions can be layered on top of them.  This way reasoning about the class invariants is easy as little code is in scope of the class's member variables.  More generally, classes should be open for extension but closed for modification.  So the interface should be sufficient to allow functions like this to be written as non-friend globals.

Since mxnet 1.3.1, cpu with dropout is supported in mx.symbol.RNN, which provides a more user friendly support for RNN. For example, the whole architecture could be built from scratch with: 

```
data <- mx.symbol.Variable("data")
label <- mx.symbol.Variable("label")

data <- mx.symbol.swapaxes(data, dim1 = 0, dim2 = 1)
RNN <- mx.symbol.RNN(data = data, 
                     state_size = 50, 
                     num_layers = 1,
                     p = 0.2,
                     state_outputs = T,
                     mode = "lstm")
data <- mx.symbol.swapaxes(data = RNN[[1]], dim1 = 0, dim2 = 1)
decode <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data = data, num_hidden = 1, flatten = F)
loss <- mx.symbol.LinearRegressionOutput(data = decode, label = label)
```

I think the above would provide a less intimidating introduction to RNN.

In `celery`, it appears the Strategy's `task_message_handler()` is responsible for scheduling the ETA request by using the `call_at()` function, which exists in some sort of timer object. I assume this timer object keeps track of the request and handles it at the appropriate time.

For `celery-batches` to do the same thing in its Strategy, it would need some similar method of tracking the request until the ETA time has passed. It seems the only way `celery-batches` can track requests over time is via its Queue. I could move the ETA logic to the Strategy, but I'm not sure what I would do with the request if its ETA time hasn't passed yet and it needs to be stored somewhere that isn't the Queue (since the Queue would only be for requests that are ready).

I think it makes sense to keep the ETA logic in `consume_queue()` and use the Queue as both temporary storage for requests that aren't ready, and temporary storage for requests that are ready. But I'm fairly new to both Celery and celery-batches, so I'm open to changing it if you think it makes sense.

Here, it is not possible, because only `mod.f`  (a function) is unpickled, so a dynamic module is never unpickled.
Creating a new module as I said does not work though, because it will only be referenced in _dynamic_modules_globals, however, since it is a `WeakValueDictionnary` , the module will be garbage collected as soon as we get out of the function where it is created.

In the current PR code, things work because ` f.__globals__ ` is a reference to a value in `_dynamic_modules_globals`, the item in this dict was not garbage collected as long as the function was not deleted, which is not the case anymore if we use a module, as f does not have a reference to the module itself.

So in this particular use case, it seems hard to keep a reference to the dynamic module where an unpickled function lived.

The conversion between angles and meters is a little goofy in these cases. The function treats the data as points on a grid; the second angle is used to find the length of the hypotenuse from (0,0) to (25067.525,25067.525), while the first angle is used to find the x and y components of said hypotenuse. Thus pixel_size=(45, -89.681194) means that x_size = cos(45)*sin((-89.681194+90)/2)*12742456 and y_size = sin(45)*sin((-89.681194+90)/2)*12742456 where 12742456 is the max distance. Note: all these angles are in degrees.

There's a minor issue here.

In the [snowball manual](https://snowballstem.org/compiler/snowman.html), `hop AE` is documented as raising signal `f` if `AE` is negative, but currently Ada fails on `hop -1` with:

```
Execution of ./bin/stemwords terminated by unhandled exception
raised CONSTRAINT_ERROR : stemmer-yiddish.adb:1045 range check failed
```

This was flagged up by testing a merge with current git master as the recently merged Yiddish algorithm currently relies on `hop -1` currently being incorrectly implemented in existing backends to move the cursor back.  We're working on fixing this algorithm and the existing backends, so Ada should handle this case by raising `f` as documented rather than following how the other backends currently behave.

So either parameter `N` here and in the backwards variant should accept signed values, with its sign checked inside, or there should be a sign check before the call (which I suppose may make it easier for the compiler to optimise the check away in the common case where `skip` is used with a constant argument).

Hi Ruben - I see the appeal for using np.nan to reflect the fact that data is missing. However, nan-type is not expected in most image processing pipelines/software and leads to unexpected behavior downstream, the reason which may not be obvious for the average user (see below). E.g. many numpy operations with np.nan yield nan: 

```
>>> n = np.nan
>>> s = [3,27,np.nan,93.3]

>>> print(np.divide(100,n))
>>> print(np.add(100,n))
>>> print(np.power(100,n))
>>> print(np.mean(s))
>>> print(np.max(s))
nan
nan
nan
nan
nan
```
One more concrete example: a common operation in image-processing is to re-scale intensity to fall between 0-1, or to normalize image values to have zero-mean. Both operations require division by image max and mean respectively. If max/mean is calculated across a dimension that contains `nan`, all values within the stack will convert to `nan`, effectively deleting all data. This happens e.g. when calling` skimage.exposure.rescale_intensity()` since image min and max are calculated by numpy functions: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/d8f23c64c45427962ec5e7f975140de2d726547d/skimage/exposure/exposure.py#L258-L259

The same risk exists for virtually all arithmetic operations called on arrays that include nan-types. 

**In effect, serving nan-type will force most users to manually convert** `nan ` **to zeros before running computations.** I think missing frames are handled better as zeros than nan-type. Most packages for writing image-files generally convert nan-type to zeros as well.

LMK what you think.

>what if we persisted every snapshot?

Multiple problems I think:

1. Lot's of overhead in snapshot creation
2. Still one db lookup per block to get the snapshot
3. Lots of wasted disk space that we need to clean up again

>Much like how it passes in the DB, but with the expectation that everything in the volatile store will be dropped on shutdown.

Yes, dropping the data on shutdown won't be a problem I think even dropping them between forks wouldn't be a problem at all. It would just be one header after the fork that causes us to suck in 30000 headers from the db until we are back to normal.

So, yeah, that would work but I think it can not be just some predefined storage because e.g. in this case we want to setup an LRU cache for the snapshots and in other cases we might needs some different kind of storage or eviction polity.

But actually, this brought me to some idea and I took a stab at it and it seems to work. Here's a rough outline and then I continue with some inline comments.

Instead of the `ConsensusAPI` taking in the db, it will take in a `ConsensusContextAPI`.

```python
class ConsensusAPI(ABC):

    @abstractmethod
    def __init__(self, context: ConsensusContextAPI) -> None:
        """
        Initialize the consensus api.
        """
        ...
```

We set the `consensus_context_class` on the chain. And just like the `ChainDB` the `consensus_context` is set *once* when the chain is initialized.

```python
class Chain(BaseChain):
    ...
    chaindb_class: Type[ChainDatabaseAPI] = ChainDB
    consensus_context_class: Type[ConsensusContextAPI] = ConsensusContext

    def __init__(self, base_db: AtomicDatabaseAPI) -> None:
       ...
        self.chaindb = self.get_chaindb_class()(base_db)
        self.consensus_context = self.consensus_context_class(self.chaindb.db)
        self.headerdb = HeaderDB(base_db)
```

Then later, in `get_vm(at_header)` that context is being used.

```python
    def get_vm(self, at_header: BlockHeaderAPI = None) -> VirtualMachineAPI:
        header = self.ensure_header(at_header)
        vm_class = self.get_vm_class_for_block_number(header.block_number)
        chain_context = ChainContext(self.chain_id)

        return vm_class(
            header=header,
            chaindb=self.chaindb,
            chain_context=chain_context,
            consensus_context=self.consensus_context
        )
```

So, the bottom line is:

- we continue to have VM instances that do only operate on the state that they get passed in
- we have consensus context state that is per-chain rather than per-vm
- we do not have any state leaking (as in: two different chain instances accidentially sharing consensus state).

There's a lot to clean up here but you can take a look at [the most recent commit](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/1899/commits/6e8484ba6475433280119167b884c76de8b594fd) which implements this and also another one [on the Trinity side](https://github.com/ethereum/trinity/pull/1196) that takes it on a ride. It works and I think it kinds gives us the best of both worlds.

There are a few methods I use to resolve circular imports.

1) Shared private attribute. So, for example there'd be a tag property such as `self._can_cast_ref` that can be used without importing anything. I don't exactly like this approach here.
2) Private tag type that can be imported standalone that the types inherit from. Basically the same as #1 except using inheritance and `isinstance` instead of flimsy attributes. See, e.g. `BaseActivity` or `_EmojiTag` for examples in the library.

The decision to change the API was made offline. If I remember correctly, this was related to @bdice request to move error checking from cython to C++.
When constructing NeighborLists using `from_points`, the current API does error checking for index lengths in C++. If we revert `compute_distances(query_points, points)`, one of three things needs to happen with the NeighborList's `from_points` function:
1) Error checking happens in Cython before calling `compute_distances`
2) Let `compute_distances` throw it's error about `query_points` and `points` lengths not matching. The user can infer that the indicies for either do not match.
3) A new function is written in C++ to check this error.

The system provides its status using quite opaque types (both short and long identifiers, lists etc.).  I think having one function understand the tools output is valuable rather than having to query the system status three times to get the required information from the same output, even though it has to handle three types of data in one output.

I guess putting this into a named and typed data structure rather than a ``Dict`` can have value.  You would still need to read the code defining that data structure to understand it and use it, but it will undeniably be more clear.

The flip side is that it would require maintenance to the library any/every time the upstream project adds information to the output, but I guess it does make sense to handle that at the core, where there also is expert knowledge about what every item really means as opposed to letting downstream code cope with it as best it can.

`time_open_days` is not correctly calculated, since this field should be updated every day. The way of achieving this is by using a scripted field in the dashboard.

Imagine that we are fetching issues from the ubuntu issue tracker. In the first execution, `time_open_days` will be correctly calculated, since it will be derived by comparing the issue creation date and the day when the execution was made. However this field will be recalculated only when the issue is updated upstream. Thus, the `time_open_days` won't reflect the metric `time_open_days`, but just the time when the issue was last updated.

The actual problem is that the `FileNotFoundError` exception text does not contain the name of the file that is not found:
```
>>> ssl_cxt.load_verify_locations('xx')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory
```

Same for `PermissionError`.

That makes it hard to find out from the exception message whether certfile, keyfile, or cafile had an issue. (Note: Unfortunately, a missing or unpermitted capath directory gets raised as an issue only later).
 
If you specify your own exception classes, the file name can be part of the exception message.

For example:

```
class PrometheusClientError:
    pass

class PrometheusClientSSLError(PrometheusClientError, ssl.SSLError):
    pass
    
class PrometheusClientFileNotFoundError(PrometheusClientError, FileNotFoundError):
    pass
    
class PrometheusClientPermissionError(PrometheusClientError, PermissionError):
    pass
```

and then:
```
def _get_ssl_ctx(

    . . .

    if cafile is not None or capath is not None:
        try:
            ssl_cxt.load_verify_locations(cafile, capath)
        except IOError as exc:
            exc_type = type(exc)
            msg = str(exc)
            raise exc_type(f"Cannot load CA certificate chain from file {cafile!r} or directory {capath!r}: {msg}")
    else:
        try:
            ssl_cxt.load_default_certs(purpose=ssl.Purpose.CLIENT_AUTH)
        except IOError as exc:
            exc_type = type(exc)
            msg = str(exc)
            raise exc_type(f"Cannot load default CA certificate chain: {msg}")

    . . .

    try:
        ssl_cxt.load_cert_chain(certfile=certfile, keyfile=keyfile)
        except IOError as exc:
            exc_type = type(exc)
            msg = str(exc)
            raise exc_type(f"Cannot load default server certificate file {certfile!r} or its private key file {keyfile!r}: {msg}")    
```

It is worth pointing out that `cache.clear()` issues a `SCAN 0 MATCH *` against the selected DB, based on the `LOCATION` of the django cache.   This means it will find all keys for cache aliases that share the `LOCATION`.

For example - let's say I have a brand new redis with no data in it, and i have two caches (c1, c2) defined with the same location.

```
>>> caches['c1'].set('foo', 'bar')
True
>>> caches['c2'].set('foo', 'bar')
True
>>> caches['c2'].clear()
2
```

from the redis `MONITOR`:

```
+1620157208.407551 [9 172.20.0.1:62058] "SCAN" "0" "MATCH" "*"
+1620157208.409341 [9 172.20.0.1:62058] "DEL" "c1:None:foo"
+1620157208.411038 [9 172.20.0.1:62058] "DEL" "c1:None:foo"
```


Does this mean this PR is wrong?  no, but `cache[<alias>].clear()` does more than what you'd expect.

I am now quite convinced that the `pypi` protocol can be an external library that is re-usable.  It could even avoid the `pip install` "bug" https://github.com/moremoban/moban/issues/278 by doing a `pip download --only-binary :all:` (wheel fetch only) of the name/version/etc, and then use `archivefs` on the fetched file which is a wheel and thus has a known internal structure.

e.g. `pypi://pypi-mobans-pkg==1.1/resources/foo/bar.txt`

Note this `pypi://` opener also could give access to git repos, as `pip download` can also fetch vcs links as long as they have a `setup.py` or `pyproject.toml` .

The url for that could be `pypi+git://github.com/mobans/pypi-mobans-pkg@1.1/resources/foo/bar.txt` and `pypi+svn://github.com/mobans/pypi-mobans-pkg@15/resources/foo/bar.txt`

(I am not sure about that `+` -- need to check if pyfilesystem2 supports that.)

This is extra reason to not use the protocol name `pypi:` now , as its meaning will change if we do implement the above (which would remove the need for `requires:`).

We can theoretically have thousands of trials. The rationale here is that we add trials to `self._pending_trials`, which is a set and O(1), and to `self._pending_trials_list`, where insertion is O(1) and deletion is O(n). Instead of deleting from the list, we just iterate over the list and discard any objects that are not in the set.
The reason that we don't iterate over the set is because we want to keep the order the scheduler gave us intact.

Hmmm, the [API docs for the `Object` representation](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#crc32c) state:
```
CRC32c checksum, as described in RFC 4960, Appendix B; encoded using base64 in big-endian byte order. For more information about using the CRC32c checksum, see Hashes and ETags: Best Practices.
```

[Likewise for `md5hash`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#md5hash):
```
MD5 hash of the data; encoded using base64. For more information about using the MD5 hash, see Hashes and ETags: Best Practices.
```

The `Blob.crc32c` and `Blob.md5_hash` properties don't do any decoding / remapping of the `base64`-encoded strings (which is maybe a pain for users), but leaving the `==` padding characters in place looks consistent with how we normally store those values.

Not sure if I got the indents right, but the idea is that once we've found a NaN the following values don't matter anymore and we can break out of the two innermost loops early. `not min_value < tmp` will be True for NaNs as well as for smaller values. The reason the `break` statement is guarded by the `if not` statement is that I expect it to be False more often than True -> we find a new `min_value` less often than we reject the current `tmp` value; is that reasonable?

Even if this is reasonably fast, we might still go for your suggestion with two kernels. It's a great idea and the code of each kernel is straight forward and probably easier to maintain longterm than my suggestion. And we can still use the idea of breaking out of the 2 inner loops early once we find a NaN!

```suggestion
        with open(datafile, "rt", encoding="UTF-8") as file:
```

Even if the file happens to be pure-ASCII, as mentioned previously this still needlessly breaks the tests on any system where the encoding returned by `locale.getpreferredencoding()` doesn't happen to be a strict superset of ASCII. This is the case on many Windows systems by default, e.g. for any system set to use a Japanese locale this will default to Shift-JIS, which differs from ASCII in not containing the tilde `~` and backslash `\` characters, both of which are present in these files.

Furthermore, the encoding of the input files is explicitly known (UTF-8, as required by the JSON standard and universal in practice), so it should be explicitly specified rather relying on arbitrary, environment-specific configuration—per the zen of Python, "explicit is better than implicit". And on a practical note, I find that keeping a consistent good habit of always explicitly passing the encoding of text-mode files (either as a known value or detected/inferred via some other means) takes way less effort than reasoning out every time whether it would happen to work without it, not to mention eliminates the possibility of making a mistake in that determination :)

> avoid encoding quantiles in strings

Let's start with [this simpler conversation](https://github.com/mozilla/mozanalysis/pull/44/files/af80b1ce1476f54ddfa5197cd38dae78860e64f8#r288797470)

> avoid abbreviations

Would you prefer `relative_uplift_*` and `absolute_uplift_*`? What about `maximum_absolute_difference_*`? Bear in mind that the longer the label is, the more likely that it gets truncated with the value missing (so we can't distinguish `relative_uplift_0.005` from `relative_uplift_0.995`)... 

> using a Series here doesn't feel natural to me

I was tossing up between a dict or Series. Here are the features that drove me to use a Series:
* I want values to be ordered (grouping related quantities together and ordering withing those groups) and keyed
* I want the keys to be flexible (an arbitrary list of quantiles might be supplied)
* Batch mode will smash a bunch of these return values together, and the user should be able to conveniently address the collection of results either by row or column (i.e. get all quantiles for one set of samples, or get one quantile across all sets of samples). To me this really smells like a DataFrame.

Does that go some way to convince you that a Series is better here than an OrderedDict, dict or custom type? 

> Am I right that most users most of the time will probably care about the (0.025, 0.975) interval for a 95% CI?

Quite possibly? Personally I like to present 90% and 99% CIs/probability bands, to communicate the fuzziness of results and discourage premature "binarification" of outcomes; but this might not catch on and the function should default to what the team would choose to use if they had a long and informed discussion. Personally I would like to continue using 90% and 99% CIs/probability bands (unless there's a reason why they're problematic) and I would ideally like this function to support that, though it does not need to be the default.

Also I don't know whether people care more about absolute uplift or relative uplift; personally I've found relative uplift to be more useful most of the time, but there have been cases when absolute uplift has added perspective (e.g. when you forget that you're looking at a 0.01% absolute change on a very low baseline and you needed a 10% absolute change). So I threw both in here.

> I wonder if it doesn't make sense to spec these in terms of the interval width rather than the individual quantiles.

When I apply this function to samples from a posterior probability distribution, I like communicating to stakeholders that "There is a 5% chance that the metric will drop by more than `res.loc[rel_uplift_0.05]`" so I'd like the labels to reference the individual quantiles. If you think it makes more sense to specify interval width and have it spit out quantile labels then sure, I'm not overly fussed. I defaulted to having the argument more directly reflect the labels, but it does add a probably-unnecessary degree of freedom. Have your thoughts wandered to anywhere stronger than "wonder"?

```python
import importlib

importlib.import_module("ujson.dumps")
# ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ujson.dumps'; 'ujson' is not a package
```

This because `ujson.dumps` is a function, not a module. A solution could be:
```python
import importlib

if serializer is None and custom_serializer is not None:
        try:
            # user provided a module path, attempt to import the 'dumps' method of the module
            mod = importlib.import_module(custom_serializer)
            if hasattr(mod, "dumps"):
                serializer = getattr(mod, "dumps")
        except ImportError:   
             if "." in custom_serializer:
                # user may have provided a path to a function
                # attempt to import the module and retrieve the function attribute
                parts = custom_serializer.split(".")
                mod_path = "".join(parts[:-1])
                func_name = parts[-1]
                try:
                    mod = importlib.import_module(mod_path)
                    if hasattr(mod, func_name):
                        serializer = getattr(mod, func_name)
                except ImportError:
                    pass
    elif serializer is None:
        serializer = dumps
```

I'm a bit hesitant about the idea of tracking file extensions. A file may have the wrong ending or no ending at all, and the only way to identify it is via its content. We can still do caching based on file type to speed up routing once it becomes a bottleneck; however, I think we should keep the option to let a plugin decide what it can or cannot open and allow it to check more than just the extension if it sees fit.

Another consideration about having a class attribute with supported extensions is that we will run in periodic issues, where a plugin does support a format, but `imageio` doesn't because the tag is missing in the class attribute (#604, #586, #596, etc.). I'd prefer us not to track supported formats, which seems more smooth when upstream upgrades and supports new formats.

Think about this from the types perspective: there is a type `List[T]` for a list of objects of type `T`; then there is also type `Tuple[T1, T2, ..., Tn]` for a tuple of objects of types `T1`, `T2`, ..., `Tn`. So it is natural to use lists for a sequence of same-typed elements of arbitrary length, and it is natural to use tuples for a fixed-length sequence of items of different types.

Though in python it is possible to put anything into a list, or to create a tuple of any length -- this is much less natural, and goes against principles of other typed languages.

It could be argued though that `DT.to_list()` should really return a tuple of columns instead of a list, but I'm not sure if it's worth it...

the need to pass in a `topic` 'cause of SR kind of ruins the api from the perspective of being able to beautifully specify callables inline. Furthermore, I think you'll also find that this isn't something you can do in most cases anyway. For example, how easy is it to write a bigendian/littleendian architecture aware integer serializer? I haven't looked into this in python, but i suspect it's something you wouldn't define inline (and probably something we'll provide with the library). So this idea - although very attractive (i was also attracted to it initially) - is the wrong thing to be prioritizing. what you have is kind of a middle ground that isn't optimal in any way.

given the above, I think the most important thing to be optimizing on is the point of view of someone using an existing serializer, and the callable interface should be extended to be completely general - include everything that could ever possibly be wanted (more than topic), including `is_key`. this simplifies the surface area of the parts of the library users will see/use in most cases. it's also allows for maximum flexibility. and it's also not a half-arsed middle ground - it's taking a point of view that is well justified.

I'm not sure; I guess it depends on the desired interface contract. The changeset as it is now proposes that this API be a relatively thin wrapper for server shutdown, i.e. changing the state of the server without changing the state in the client (tearing down the connection). I feel that destroying the client (modifying the client state) should be done explicitly at the callsite with an subsequent `quit` or similar.

The semantics of this new API could also be changed to mean "invoke both client and server shutdown" but that conflates the responsibilities I think. For example, imagine the case where you deploy memcached behind a protocol-aware proxy (which we do); you might not necessarily want the client<>proxy connection destroyed after relaying the shutdown command between proxy<>server(s).

There can be several work units that have not been completed, i.e. the current work unit and its parents. The collection tracks the increments on the work unit which was active at the time (keyed on the span ID). Counter increments that occurred in a parent while it was the only active work unit are only propagated on that parent work unit when it completes.

The work unit is stored on the stack and I'm not sure how I would access it safely from other code without wrapping it in some sort of smart pointer with locking. https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/9195e6023c428dfc80b7b1aef1e929fdd00625a6/src/rust/engine/workunit_store/src/lib.rs#L740

It seemed more straightforward to store the temporary state of counters on the `WorkunitStore` itself (via the `MetricsData` struct). That is already accessible in other task contexts. You will note that this pattern is already followed in how we send work units through channels. The data for the work unit is sent, not the work unit itself. So I just followed the existing pattern.

> I guess you could decide that user has to choose something: leaving both password and prompt unset would be an error. Then calling the function with default argument values would fail with ValueError instead of prompting unexpectedly.

Do you mean?:
```
(password=None, prompt=None)  --> error (default)
(password=None, prompt=True)  --> prompt
(password=None, prompt=False) --> plain
(password="..", prompt=None)  --> encrypt
(password="..", prompt=True)  --> error
(password="..", prompt=False) --> encrypt
```
I think we can do that, but I'm not a big fan of raising an exception per default, nor of using 3 values for a boolean.

> To create unencrypted keys you would have to generate_and_write_rsa_keypair(filepath, prompt=False): it's not ideal because nothing in that explicitly shows that the result is going to be unencrypted... so it's still not great.

If we go with above suggestion, I think it makes more sense to use `encrypt` instead of `prompt`. 

> I guess a combo is possible as well: user needs to choose exactly one of password=<value>, prompt=True or encrypted=False -- then each call would be explicit about what it wants. In another language this would be a SecurityType enum with three possible choices but that seems a bit clunky in Python

That  -- i.e.  pass a subclass of `KeySerializationEncryption`, e.g. `BestAvailableEncryption(password)` or `NoEncryption` --  is what [`pyca/cryptography` does](https://cryptography.io/en/latest/hazmat/primitives/asymmetric/serialization.html#serialization-encryption-types). I wouldn't say that it is generally clunky in Python, but it doesn't really fit in with the general style of our interface.

You can actually use the built-in context manager to handle this for you. Combined with `pytest.fixture`, this will actually make your code a lot easier to manage.

e.g.

```python
@pytest.fixture
def baseline_filename():
    # create your file contents
    ...

    # then, you can write your files
    with create_file_with_content(...) as first_file, \
            create_file_with_content(...) as second_file, \
            tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as baseline_file:
        ...
        baseline.save_to_file(...)
        yield baseline_file.name

# powered by
@contextmanager
def create_file_with_content(content):
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as f:
        f.write(content)
        f.seek(0)

        yield f.name

# then
def test_generate_report(..., baseline_filename):
    output = generate_report(baseline_filename, class_to_print)
```

> If we don't store the session then the transaction will either end up as a leaked database connection (if we don't close it) or will end up closed (making the user's subsequent queries worthless).

No no, I'm not saying we shouldn't store the session. We need to. I'm just saying that we could just rely on `self._session` instead of creating a separate `session` variable and then determining whether we're in a transaction or not by checking the values of `command` and/or `self._session` as we do on lines 364 and 368. So what I'm proposing is something like this:

```
if command is BEGIN or START:
  if self._in_transaction:
    raise exception

  set self._in_transaction

...

if self._session is None:
  set self._session to a new session

execute query

if not self._in_transaction:
  commit
elif command is COMMIT or ROLLBACK:
  commit
  unset self._in_transaction
  close session

# TODO how should executing COMMIT or ROLLBACK without a BEGIN or START behave?
```

Yes, this does add an additional instance variable, but I think it perhaps makes it easier to follow what we're trying to do? Oh, we're creating a new session if we don't have one and only committing if we're not a transaction or we're done with a transaction, instead of wondering, okay what's `session` and how's that different from `self._session`? And why does `session` sometimes have a value when `self._session` is `None`? etc.

```
./pants --coverage-py-filter="v1,v2" options | grep coverage-py.filter
coverage-py.filter = ['v1,v2'] (from FLAG)
```

The only way to specify multiple options with a `list` is by either using explicit list syntax like `['v1', 'v2']` or to pass the flag multiple times. https://pants.readme.io/docs/options#list-values

@scv119 A few reasons that I didn't go that route:
1. `s.count()` can be expensive (a fan-out of tasks to compute the number of rows in each block), so we should definitely cache these values instead of recomputing them.
2. We want to batch splits if we can since these are pretty expensive (involving tasks and most likely data transfer). E.g. for the case of `[1, 1, 1, 2, 20]`, the multi-spit and union buffer that I added would result in 1 union call and 1 split_at_indices call; what you have above would result in ~7 union calls and ~7 split_at_indices calls, and the choice of _how_ the datasets are split and merged is very suboptimal.
3. The number of input splits won't equal the number of output splits if `num_splits > len(ds._blocks)` (which I believe we will want to support), so we can't replace the original splits array.

It's just a silly little optimization. The thinking was that with `.get("foo", {})`, a new `dict` is always instantiated and subsequently thrown away if the key is found. With `or`, the dict is only instantiated if the lookup fails, thanks to short-circuiting. A quick benchmark using `timeit`:

    In [1]: message = {"a": 1}
    In [2]: never_get = lambda: message.get("a", {})
    In [3]: never_or = lambda: message.get("a") or {}
    In [4]: always_get = lambda: message.get("b", {})
    In [5]: always_or = lambda: message.get("b") or {}
    In [6]: import timeit
    In [7]: timeit.timeit(never_get, setup="gc.enable()")
    Out[7]: 0.09470749995671213
    In [8]: timeit.timeit(never_or, setup="gc.enable()")
    Out[8]: 0.08076889999210835
    In [9]: timeit.timeit(always_get, setup="gc.enable()")
    Out[9]: 0.09426169993821532
    In [10]: timeit.timeit(always_or, setup="gc.enable()")
    Out[10]: 0.09646200004499406

So, using `or` is somewhat faster when the key is there most of the time. It's only a difference of ~0.01us though, so if you prefer the slightly less confusing code, I'm ok with that.

Accessing ``__all__`` is fragile (https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html?highlight=__all__#importing-from-a-package), even if in practice ``locale`` always has it.

Rather to use ``dir`` to access the attributes of ``locale``:

```
>>> print(', '.join(sorted(name for name in locale.__all__ if name.startswith('LC_'))))
LC_ALL, LC_COLLATE, LC_CTYPE, LC_MESSAGES, LC_MONETARY, LC_NUMERIC, LC_TIME

>>> print(', '.join(name for name in sorted(dir(locale)) if name.startswith('LC_')))
LC_ALL, LC_COLLATE, LC_CTYPE, LC_MESSAGES, LC_MONETARY, LC_NUMERIC, LC_TIME
```

Or, if you want to get the attribute values, we can use ``vars``:

```

> As per the ASGI specifications, any application can store custom information on the `scope`.
> To be precise, it should be stored under the `extensions` key.

Not really sure about this. As per https://github.com/encode/starlette/discussions/1511 it seems like storing non-ASGI state in `scope` is not really spec'd yet.

That's also different from [ASGI extensions](https://asgi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extensions.html) which are meant to convey information from and to the ASGI server (eg. Uvicorn).

So the current status seems to be:

* Middleware may conveniently add items to `scope` to make data available to endpoints.
* Make sure to properly namespace to avoid conflicts.
* This is mostly a convention -- not standardized yet.

So, _maybe_ the example could be more like this:

```python
scope["my-special-feature"] = {"enabled": True}
```

But it's not even clear if we _should_ do that _or_ edit `extensions` (which we'd need to `.setdefault()` since it might already be set due to other middleware...?). I think it's a safer, "sure not to interfere with other middleware" option.

```suggestion
def make_file_id(ocrd_file, output_file_grp):
    """
    Derive a new file ID for an output file from an existing input file ``ocrd_file``
    and the name of the output file's ``fileGrp/@USE``, ``output_file_grp``.
    If ``ocrd_file``'s ID contains the input file's fileGrp name, then replace it by ``output_file_grp``.
    Otherwise use ``output_file_grp`` together with the position of ``ocrd_file`` within the input fileGrp
    (as a fallback counter).
    """
    ret = ocrd_file.ID.replace(ocrd_file.fileGrp, output_file_grp)
    if ret == ocrd_file.ID:
        m = re.match(r'.*?(\d{3,}).*', ocrd_file.pageId or '')
        if m:
            n = m.group(1)
        else:
            files = ocrd_file.mets.find_files(fileGrp=ocrd_file.fileGrp, mimetype=ocrd_file.mimetype)
            n = files.index(ocrd_file)
        ret = concat_padded(output_file_grp, n)
    return ret
```

Continuing the conversation from https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/5219#discussion_r455473665:

> As for the proxied MLR.req, we analyzed the logic of sending MLR.req without using any table and found that the time complexity could be O(ChildNum * IpNumPerChild * IP6AddressesTlvSize). On the other hand, the current implementation using a table has a time complexity of O(MulticastIpNum). I think we can discuss more in the proxied MLR.req PR #5236 .

I think there are ways to reduce the overhead. For example, we could maintain a bit-vector with each bit corresponding to a child's address in the child table. A bit is set if the corresponding child's address is to be used when sending an MLR.req.

When adding a child multicast address, check to see if any of the addresses marked for use in MLR.req match (i.e. the address entries with the corresponding bit set).

1. If there is a match, clear the bit corresponding to the address being added. This indicates that the newly added address is already included in MLR.req messages.

1. If there is no match, set the bit corresponding to the address being added. This indicates that the newly added address needs to be added to MLR.req messages.

When removing a child multicast address:

1. If the corresponding bit is set, check to see if any other child has a matching address. If no matching address, clear the bit. This indicates that the address can be removed from the MLR.req message.

1. If the corresponding bit is clear, simply remove the address as this indicates that other children are still using the address.

When sending an MLR.req message, simply send the multicast addresses that have the bit set.

If we wanted to further optimize memory, then we could distribute the bit vector into the child table - I believe there is some padding that we could use to avoid any additional memory overhead. If we wanted to avoid scanning each child table entry, we could have a top-level bit vector that indicates whether a child entry has any such bit set.

Thoughts?

A NumpyArray with `len(shape) == 0` should never get to the Python level. `Content` subclasses sent to Python should pass through this `box` function ([here](https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/5f1a068fec0d00936bb353318911855788a655ec/src/python/content.cpp#L14-L130)) that turns a scalar NumpyArray (one with `len(shape) == 0`) into a Python number. The same is true of `None` (the subclass of `Content` that should turn into a Python `None`). If this is what you're seeing, the error is upstream.

All of the functions that return `Content` subclasses in src/python/content.cpp are _supposed to_ pass their output through `box`, but there isn't anything in place to ensure this is the case.

Strictly speaking, any `char*` or `std::string` from C++ ought to be a Python `bytes`: those are at the same level of abstraction. In Uproots 1‒3, they were, but users complained about the `bytes` handling, especially as Python 3 became the only Python.

So we have a policy of presenting human-readable/interpretable strings (like `fName` and `fTitle`) as `str` and binary stuff (like `fUUID`) as `bytes`. We use `"utf-8"` with `errors="surrogateescape"` as an encoding convention. It's not necessarily what the user intended—maybe they expect all of their axis labels to be in `"latin-1"`, or maybe `"latin-4"`, but UTF-8 is ASCII for ASCII and `errors="surrogateescape"` allows non-UTF-8 to at least be round-trippable:

```python
>>> b"he\xffllo".decode("utf-8")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xff in position 2: invalid start byte
```

but

```python
>>> b"he\xffllo".decode("utf-8", errors="surrogateescape")
'he\udcffllo'
```

and

```python
>>> b"he\xffllo".decode("utf-8", errors="surrogateescape").encode(errors="surrogateescape")
b'he\xffllo'
```

I think there are functions in _util.py for these conversions.

About your real question: wanting to avoid double-conversion. I just noticed that there's no `bytestring` function to complement our

https://github.com/scikit-hep/uproot5/blob/33777b16c654f1fa4c26a960af1e70f2357425d3/src/uproot/serialization.py#L19-L32

You can just add one (also in serialization.py):

```python
def bytestring(data):
    """
    Converts Python bytes into another byte sequence that is ready to be serialized
    into a file.

    If the length of the bytes object is less than 255, it is preceded by a 1-byte
    length; otherwise, it is preceded by ``b'\xff'`` and a 4-byte length.
    """
    length = len(data)
    if length < 255:
        return struct.pack(">B%ds" % length, length, data)
    else:
        return struct.pack(">BI%ds" % length, 255, length, data)
```

For the confusion with `process_global`/`process_local`, it's important to keep the name of its encapsulating class in mind: `Nrn2ArbMechGrouper`. These two methods define the interface of that class and, thus, map parameters that come in Neuron format as extracted in the first step (shared between `create_hoc` and `create_acc`) to Arbor format. The `_local` and `_global` qualifiers refer to the input arguments, thus, Neuron's representation. The output, though, is Arbor's view. The name global property is reserved in Arbor and the way to represent this in a decor is with the `default` keyword. As you can see from the invocation context, global properties obtained derived from `global_params`, `section_params` and `range_params` all get absorbed into what eventually becomes `global_mechs` and `global_scaled_mechs` in the Jinja templates.

```python
        # We bundle blocks according to the following rules:
        # 1. Attempt to bundle up to the target block size.
        # 2. If the max concurrency of the ActorPool is set, then
        #    cap the number of bundles to match the size of the ActorPool.
        #    This avoids additional overhead in submitting new actor tasks and allows
        #    the actor task to do optimizations such as batch prefetching.
        if target_block_size is None:
            target_block_size = 0
        if not math.isinf(self.max_size):
            total_size = sum(
                meta.num_rows if meta.num_rows is not None else 0
                for _, meta in blocks_in)
            pool_max_block_size = total_size // self.max_size
            target_block_size = max(target_block_size, pool_max_block_size)
        if target_block_size > 0:
            _check_target_block_size(blocks_in, target_block_size, name)
            block_bundles: List[
                Tuple[Tuple[ObjectRef[Block]], Tuple[BlockMetadata]]
            ] = _bundle_blocks_up_to_size(blocks_in, target_block_size)
```
This is basically what you have except:
1. Have `target_block_size = 0` signal no bundling, since bundling blocks to size 0 doesn't make sense and would always produce empty bundles, and map `target_block_size is None` to this.
2. If the number of rows for a block is not available, fallback to 0. Falling back to more conservative bundling when encountering an unknown sized block instead bundling all blocks into a single bundle seems better to me.
3. Check the target block size after applying the actor pool size bound.

Do you mean skip sending RS, if the info from this "router" does not impact any of the decisions (i.e., what we decide to publish in net data and/or whether or not to advertise our local on-link prefix)?

I considered something similar initially, i.e. just monitoring and sending NS to routers that are impacting some decision. This became complex and rather tricky since as things change (e.g., we receive from new router, or a prefix is removed/aged, etc), info from this router may become important and impact our decision and then we may rely on stale info (if we don't monitor the router is still present and active).

-----

A related idea/thought: If there are other Thread BRs (connected to same Thread mesh), we receive and process RAs from them, but since all prefixes are from net data or match our on-link prefix, we don't save any of the prefixes which cause the "router" entry to have no prefix and therefore removed from the list. So practically, we should not have any other Thread BRs (connected to same network) in our "router" list and won't monitor and send NS to any of them.

This policy seems to work best if a new model is loaded due to only a single request.

However, suppose a few different end users requests a new model all at once. Ideally, one replica should load that model and serve the few users, just like what happens if they send their requests one after another. Instead, those requests will be sent to different HTTP proxies, and those proxies will assign the requests to many different replicas (since no replica currently has the model loaded). All those replicas may evict their current model when loading the new model. This could cause some thrashing if there’s a couple groups of end users, each requesting a different model.

In other words, if n clients request a new model at roughly the same time, n replicas will evict their current model and load the new one. Ideally, if n is small, only 1 replica should load the model and serve all n clients.

I think this is a relatively common usage pattern, so we should be aware of it. I'm not sure if it's worth handling right now versus in the future.

Here is some (slightly) simplified code to repro the the failure. When debugging I'm seeing that [this while loop never exits](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/fac7db19a1e8e15e5b302e2203ce35cd0485bc88/src/urllib3/response.py#L726).
```
import os
import pathlib
import requests
import responses
import tempfile


def download_file(uri, handle, chunk_size):
    """Download the Bulk API result file for a single batch,
    and remove it when the context manager exits."""
    resp = requests.get(uri, headers={}, stream=True)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    f = os.fdopen(handle, "wb")

    for chunk in resp.iter_content(chunk_size=chunk_size):
        f.write(chunk)

    f.close()
    return f


@responses.activate
def test_download_file__chunk_size_one():
    url = "https://example.com"
    responses.add(method="GET", url=url, body=b"TEST\xe2\x80\x94")

    (handle, path) = tempfile.mkstemp(text=False)
    f = download_file(url, handle, chunk_size=1)
    pathlib.Path(path).unlink()


@responses.activate
def test_download_file__chunk_size_none():
    url = "https://example.com"
    responses.add(method="GET", url=url, body=b"TEST\xe2\x80\x94")

    (handle, path) = tempfile.mkstemp(text=False)
    f = download_file(url, handle, chunk_size=None)
    pathlib.Path(path).unlink()
```

I don't think this is how you access it in Python code exactly. You access an environment variable in Python code like this:

```
import os
my_env_var = os.getenv("ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLE_NAME")
```

What you have here is how you bring environment variables into Dagster config using the StringSource type. For example, If i defined an op like 

```
@op(config_schema={"param_name": StringSource})
def my_op(context):
  name = context.op_config["param_name"]
```

I could then configure it in the launchpad like this:
```
ops:
  my_op:
    config:
       param_name:
         env:
           ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLE_NAME  
```

We should separate out how to access the environment variables in Python code (which isn't particularly Dagster-specific) with how to incorporate them into the Dagster type system (which is optional and very Dagster-specific)

I don't find the source now, but the gist of the problem is:

- When Django is a dependency of a package and Django is not installed yet (!) then installing the package will install the best-matching Django version (as in "just any Django version, which satisfies the dependency I specify as a package developer").
- This can lead to a conflict when that Django version is incompatible with the explicit requirements that you have specified in your project (your project's `requirements.txt` file or similar).

Hence, it's preferred to leave the responsibility of ensuring Django is installed to the (project) developer.

I'm interested in knowing which "high quality packages" (no pun intended) specify Django as an explicit dependency, and why or whether this is not a problem. Can you post a list here?

Here, in `behave-django` we have taken a similar decision related to the [dependency on `behave`](https://github.com/behave/behave-django/blob/master/requirements.txt#L2). The reason we did this, though, is twofold:

1. Running BDD tests is a development activity, and pulling in `behave` as a dependency is less critical (if it goes wrong) than pulling in a "wrong version" unnoticed on a customer project. And the reputation of the `behave-django` package is less at stake if it causes hick-ups due to a potential side-effect (compared to "feature-focused" packages).
2. On the convenience side, I feel I'd rather specify only `behave-django` as a development dependency than `behave-django` _and_ `behave`, which only clutters the dependency list. It's still possible to override this by installing `behave` first, explicitly.

```
def f(label=None, *args, **kwargs): pass
```
`f(label=1, 3)` raises a 
```
SyntaxError: positional argument follows keyword argument
```
and `f(3, label=1)` raises a
```
TypeError: f() got multiple values for argument 'label'
```.

You could do 
```
def conditional_unique_label(*args, label = None, **kwargs):
    ...
```
or (my preference) just drop `*args` altogether.

Hey man, I actually have some concerns about doing this automatically in Dataset iterator, specifically, I think we should honor user's choice and leave the tensor on cpu if device is not set.
For 1, this matches PyTorch's mental model and default behavior if you don't explicitly call ``to(device)``.
For 2, you can't assume this is the right time to load things onto GPU, especially now that we support ``collate_fn()``. It's quite common there are still final processing and zipping before batches are trained on (if you look at huggingface's dream booth examples).
For 3, I think scripts should run deterministically. Doing this makes me feel like users will run into confusing bugs like this runs fine on my laptop, but breaks as soon as it gets submitted to a gpu cloud instance.
I don't have context or opinion about code structure / where this should live. but I would strongly suggest we don't automatically fall back to non cpu devices here.

`io_loop` argument was deprecated and removed from Tornado 5+ (http://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/releases/v5.0.0.html#tornado-ioloop):

> The io_loop argument to PeriodicCallback has been removed.
> It is now possible to create a PeriodicCallback in one thread and start it in another without passing an explicit event loop.

But it's not obvious here, because `io_loop` that we are using here was made and started in another thread via `threadloop` (https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger-client-python/blob/master/jaeger_client/local_agent_net.py#L71) and we should pass the loop explicitly.
Fortunately we are starting polling through callback of the `io_loop` (https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger-client-python/blob/master/jaeger_client/throttler.py#L103 and https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger-client-python/blob/master/jaeger_client/sampler.py#L399) and the callback will be invoked in the thread of the `io_loop`. And that's why `PeriodicCallback` will take right loop to start (it takes existing loop from current thread), instead of creating new right in main thread.

Hmm - I think you are right. `v36.2` is much higher than necessary I think, but it is quite hard to tell from the documentation and package histories that are available on the web.

Digging into this a bit more:

- per the [packaging documentation history](https://packaging.pypa.io/_/downloads/en/latest/pdf/), it looks like PEP 508 environment marker support was added in `packaging@16.0`.
- per the [setuptools history](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#id697) and in digging through the [setuptools source code](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/20.2/pkg_resources/_vendor/packaging/__about__.py), the earliest evidence of them using `packaging>=16.0` that I can see was in `v20.2` but it seems that environment markers in this version are only supported via `extras_require` (?)
- per the [setuptools history](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#id689), I believe that support for environment markers in `install_requires` was added in `v20.5`

So from what I can tell, it seems that as long as a user has a version of `setuptools>=20.5` the environment markers should be handled correctly. 

If you are comfortable with it, we can either:

- drop the `setup_requires` line, since it would seem that most projects are likely using a high enough `setuptools` to handle environment markers in `install_requires`
- adjust to `setup_requires=['setuptools>=20.5']`

Let me know which you prefer and I will make the adjustment!
